title: Charmed Life — 1 (Fragment 4)
sourceText:
The next thing he saw was that two of the letters were written in the same energetic writing as the signature. The first was dated twelve years ago, soon after his parents had been married. It said:

Dear Frank,

Now don’t get on your high horse. I only offered because I thought it might help. I still will help, in any way I can, if you let me know what I can do. I feel you have a claim on me.

Yrs ever,

Chrestomanci

The second letter was shorter:

Dear Chant,

The same to you. Go to blazes.

Chrestomanci

The third letter was dated six years ago, and it was written by someone else. Chrestomanci had only signed it.

Sir,

You were warned six years ago that something like what you relate might come to pass, and you made it quite clear that you wished for no help from this quarter. We are not interested in your troubles. Nor is this a charitable institution.

Chrestomanci

“What did your pa say to him?” Mrs. Sharp wondered, curious and awestruck. “Well—what do you think, dearie?”

Gwendolen held her hands spread out above the letters, rather as if she was warming them at a fire. Both her little fingers twitched. “I don’t know. They feel important—especially the first one and the last one—awfully important.”

“Who’s Chrestomanci?” Cat asked. It was a hard name to say. He said it in pieces, trying to remember the way Mrs. Sharp had said it: KREST—OH—MAN—SEE. “Is that the right way?”

“Yes, that’s right—and never you mind who he is, my love,” said Mrs. Sharp. “And important’s a weak word for it, dearie. I wish I knew what your pa had said. Something not many people’d dare say, by the sound of it. And look what he got in return! Three genuine signatures! Mr. Nostrum would give his eyes for those, dearie. Oh, you’re in luck! He’ll teach you for those all right! So would any necromancer in the country.”

Gleefully, Mrs. Sharp began packing the things away in the box again. “What have we here?” A little red book of matches had fallen out of the bundle of business letters. Mrs. Sharp took it up carefully and, quite as carefully, opened it. It was less than half full of flimsy cardboard matches. But three of the matches had been burned, without being torn out of the book first. The third one along was so very burned that Cat supposed it must have set light to the other two.

“Hm,” said Mrs. Sharp. “I think you’d better keep this, dearie.” She passed the little red book to Gwendolen, who put it in the pocket of her dress along with the earrings. “And what about you having this, my love?” Mrs. Sharp said to Cat, remembering that he had a claim too. She gave him the spray of white heather. Cat wore it in his buttonhole until it fell to pieces.

• • •

words.definition: Showing or involving great activity or vitality.
words.example: Two of the letters were written in the same energetic writing as the signature.
words.exampleText: Two of the letters were written in the same energetic writing as the signature.

words.definition: A person's name written in a distinctive way as a form of identification.
words.example: The letters were written in the same energetic writing as the signature.
words.exampleText: The letters were written in the same energetic writing as the signature.

words.definition: Marked with a date indicating when it was written or created.
words.example: The first was dated twelve years ago.
words.exampleText: The first was dated twelve years ago.

words.definition: A demand or request for something considered one's due.
words.example: I feel you have a claim on me.
words.exampleText: I feel you have a claim on me.

words.definition: Truly what something is said to be; authentic or sincere.
words.example: Three genuine signatures!
words.exampleText: Three genuine signatures!

words.definition: A person who practices necromancy, a form of magic involving communication with the dead.
words.example: So would any necromancer in the country.
words.exampleText: So would any necromancer in the country.

words.definition: To put something into a box or container for storage or transportation.
words.example: Mrs. Sharp began packing the things away in the box again.
words.exampleText: Mrs. Sharp began packing the things away in the box again.

words.definition: Comparatively light and insubstantial; easily damaged.
words.example: Less than half full of flimsy cardboard matches.
words.exampleText: Less than half full of flimsy cardboard matches.

words.definition: Damaged or marked by fire or heat.
words.example: Three of the matches had been burned.
words.exampleText: Three of the matches had been burned.

words.definition: To make a sudden small movement or jerk.
words.example: Both her little fingers twitched.
words.exampleText: Both her little fingers twitched.

words.definition: Filled with or expressing awe.
words.example: Mrs. Sharp wondered, curious and awestruck.
words.exampleText: Mrs. Sharp wondered, curious and awestruck.

words.definition: Eager to know or learn something.
words.example: Mrs. Sharp wondered, curious and awestruck.
words.exampleText: Mrs. Sharp wondered, curious and awestruck.

words.definition: A phrase indicating the letter was created twelve years prior to now.
words.example: The first was dated twelve years ago.
words.exampleText: The first was dated twelve years ago.

words.word: multi-word expression: get on your high horse
words.definition: To act arrogantly or self-righteously.
words.example: Now don’t get on your high horse.
words.exampleText: Now don’t get on your high horse.

words.definition: To ignite something on fire.
words.example: It must have set light to the other two.
words.exampleText: It must have set light to the other two.

words.definition: To inform someone in advance about a possible danger or problem.
words.example: You were warned six years ago.
words.exampleText: You were warned six years ago.

words.definition: To have a desire to know something; to be curious.
words.example: Mrs. Sharp wondered, curious and awestruck.
words.exampleText: Mrs. Sharp wondered, curious and awestruck.

words.definition: Of great significance or value.
words.example: They feel important—especially the first one and the last one.
words.exampleText: They feel important—especially the first one and the last one.

words.definition: Authentic and real, not counterfeit or fake.
words.example: Three genuine signatures!
words.exampleText: Three genuine signatures!

words.definition: As a reciprocal action or consequence.
words.example: Look what he got in return!
words.exampleText: Look what he got in return!

words.definition: A written message sent to someone.
words.example: Two of the letters were written in the same energetic writing.
words.exampleText: Two of the letters were written in the same energetic writing.

words.definition: To assist or be of service.
words.example: I still will help, in any way I can.
words.exampleText: I still will help, in any way I can.

words.definition: An organization founded for a specific purpose, especially public or charitable.
words.example: Nor is this a charitable institution.
words.exampleText: Nor is this a charitable institution.

words.definition: To tell or narrate; to give an account of.
words.example: Something like what you relate might come to pass.
words.exampleText: Something like what you relate might come to pass.

words.definition: The process of combustion producing flames and heat.
words.example: Warming them at a fire.
words.exampleText: Warming them at a fire.

words.definition: To express in words; to speak.
words.example: ‘What did your pa say to him?’
words.exampleText: ‘What did your pa say to him?’

words.definition: An expression telling someone to go away angrily or dismissively.
words.example: The same to you. Go to blazes.
